text:
If recent grads are so much worse, why is the bar so much higher?
I keep hearing senior engineers say today's CS graduates are weaker than their generation. Fine. But in 2018 you could land a $130k FAANG job with two leetcode easys, no projects and a 2.8 GPA from a mid school. Today you need four rounds of mediums and hards, multiple internships and three substantial projects just to get screened for $60k at a company nobody's heard of.
If the 2018 cohort was so talented, why did the bar reflect the opposite? Most of those "superior" engineers wouldn't survive today's process cold. The bar didn't rise because grads got worse it rose because grad got better.
label: opinion

text: Supply & demand. The bar raised mainly because companies are shedding workers en masse now that interest rates are higher, and to a lesser extent due to AI. They'll need new blood eventually, but supply & demand will rule your entire career. That's why I'd focus on learning in-demand skills that others aren't, and that aren't so easily outsourced.
label: opinion

text:
I give up - software was my biggest mistake in life so far
Despite what may seem from the text I am not suicidal, just regretting a lot. 

I give up. 

Getting into software engineering was my biggest mistake in life so far. I grieve what my life could have been if I had chosen something else. 

I have developed an interest in programming early. Most of my childhood I struggled socially so that's what I did a lot. Went through it all - Python, Arduino starter kit, daily-driving Linux, learning C.

Got my first internship at 17, a good company, not FAANG, but a major player in its field. Same year I started university, of course a CS degree which I have since changed to part-time and remote form of study to work more. Went full time at the same company. 

Despite all that I was never good-good - at this point I was programming for five years and had one semi-complete personal project, didn't want to sit down and study architecture, algorithms, smart words, best practices, didn't contribute to open source, hadn't done a single leetcode problem, didn't want to learn my tools - shortcuts, shell, build systems, and I never participated in any events, won awards, or even cared to. I was not, as kids say, cracked. 

By the time I got this internship I stopped programming for myself entirely. Back then I thought it was because I got a job, but now I recognise that me meeting new people, taking up analogue hobbies, and starting to take me playing an instrument more seriously might have been the real reason.

And when I went full time I did so knowing I have no passion, and even no real interest in software engineering. In my life I have met people who did. Who were happy to spend their weekend evening trying out a new tool which name I didn't know and which function didn't understand. I want one of those people. Never was. And I was fine with it and with working being indifferent just to have money for things I actually love. 

Then AI came. At first I disregarded it, then I began to hate it for many reasons you all heard many times now. Ever since 2022 I had made total of three prompts - one when ChatGPT came out to see what it is, and two for an assignment where it was required. 

The coding models came. My colleagues started using them. I didn't. To this day every line of code I have ever commited, is written by me, my own hands, and my own mind. they started pushing me AI-generated (or "AI-assisted") merge requests and for the first time despite my hatred of reading code I reviewed diligently to try to find a major flaw a senior develop has missed. I wanted to have an argument as to why AI had no place in our work. Maybe I am just bad, but I didn't find one. I had to approve. Then the next one, and the next. I didn't care for finding anything anymore. What's the point, it's good enough. 

Indifference turned into disdain. 

Then I thought. For the first time I thought what did I like in IT, and the only thing I was able to come up with was translating the idea of what has to be done into code. I didn't care for designing the systems, I hated reading other people's code, even when some can find beauty in it. It was the only thing that managed to bring me a bit of joy in this field of work. And now it was the first thing to be gone if I was to start using AI. It took the only thing that made it bearable from me.

I am typing this sitting at work, feeling pressure to start using the technology I hate in the profession I have to tell myself "I will make it through today" just to start the work day, fully understanding that I might become unemployable because I don't even know what an "AI agent" or "an orchestrator" is. Dreading each meeting for the fear of hearing about AI getting better again. 

I grieve what my life could have been if I had chosen something else. Getting into software engineering was my biggest mistake in life so far. 

I give up.
label: rant

text:
Vibe coding as a CS major doesn't make sense
Let's be completely real here. If you vibe code - like truly vibe code - as a student in this major, it might be time to find a new home.

By vibe coding, I mean genuinely writing little to no code by hand and letting your AI do it instead. Pretending to be an orchestrator while the AI does all the real engineering. Not struggling through a problem, and instead mistaking speed for competence.

Most of the people that I know who do this say that it's to "get comfortable with agentic coding". But the result is catastrophic: You're paying companies to do the job that you want to get paid to do.

The standard defence of industry professionals is that AI can't be a real software engineer because the experience of building things on your own gives you judgement and skill that are impossible to replicate with AI. Judgement and skill that can even be used to guide AI agents if willing. But if you spend your college days skirting around experience by outsourcing to an AI, you have no value. There is nothing that you have that an AI doesn't. You don't build the judgment, you don't build the experience, you don't build the intuition for what does and doesn't smell.

Even worse is the fact that you lose the ability to make new things. AI isn't actually able to do something that has never been done. It can pretend it does by hacking together a bunch of stuff that has been built, but this isn't true ingenuity and leads to significant technical debt. You lose the most valuable human resource in this field: ingenuity.

If you're vibe coding in college, you are genuinely utterly cooked. It is impossible to justify your value to this industry.
label: opinion

text:
Where is the evidence that the hiring bar is significantly higher now?
I’m not a new grad, but I’ve been doing interview prep for mid-level roles and I do competitive programming as a hobby so I’m aware of the sort of the questions that come up on interviews these days.

Other than that one FAANG company in 2020-2022 doing the stupid 1 round new grad interview, I’ve seen no evidence myself that the bar is significantly higher.

There are just fewer jobs. So, each step of the application process, you’re more likely to be ghosted.

I conduct interviews for new grads and mid level SDE at that aforementioned company. We’ve always had the lowest interview bar amongst FAANG, generally speaking. We still have like a ~10 percent pass rate for new grads and ~5.

If we notably increased the bar, we just wouldn’t be able to find candidates. Even in this market. So, instead the bar is retained, and more people just get ghosted.
label: question

text: Not understanding how to program well goes much deeper than lacking domain/language specific knowledge; the hard part was never the syntax. The entire crux of programming with LLMs is having the depth of knowledge to know what output is good/scalable/sensical/extensible. I do think AI will continue to be available in certain interview rounds, but betting on it becoming the only standard that candidates are graded on is incredibly risky. If we’re at the point where any layman can generate ok looking code without understanding it, wouldn’t that create more incentive for companies to verify that you can also do it by hand?

text:
Anyone else find that using AI less actually keeps them more productive?
Currently interning as a SWE this summer. I use Claude Code on the side, but I’ve found I like doing a decent amount of the implementation myself instead of having AI generate everything.

Part of it is not wanting to burn through all my Claude tokens, but the bigger reason is that it keeps me in a flow state. When I’m just prompting AI over and over and reviewing the output, it starts to feel like pulling a slot machine lever. It’s efficient, but I find it kinda boring, and I also feel like things can get messy faster.

Even if doing more myself makes me a little slower in the short term, I feel more engaged, understand the code better, and stay motivated longer.

I know this might sound obvious, but does anyone else feel this way? Not necessarily because of learning, but because coding a decent amount yourself keeps you more locked in and motivated?
label: question

text:
In my experience, projects matter to the extent that they are something you genuinely care about, worked on and improved over time, and a plus if there is a natural and genuine user base (even if it’s something silly that you use with only your friends).
In my opinion, a less “impressive” web app you created because you care about it and want to do [insert hobby] with friends will move the needle more than some super fancy IOS app that showcases a number of technologies, but that you create for your resume and then move on and never tough again, but obviously depending on company and interviewer/recruiter your mileage may vary.
Internships are good to show professional experiences and to get your resume into an interview but having real projects you’ve worked on over time and that you’re invested in I feel like can make a good thing to talk about in interviews.
It is hard to predict to what extent Leetcode will stick around but you’d probably rather spend some time learning it needlessly than write it off and fail OAs.
My only other piece of advice is keep an eye out on job postings early, such as August or September and apply ASAP. As a point of reference, for my new grad offer I applied and had first interview in September, and had last interview in December.
When all the new grads met together, everyone I talked to had a similar timeline. Many of my friends didn’t even start recruiting until January which made them logistically not have the opportunity to recruit where I did, and I imagine other “top” companies.
Similarly, applying early is helpful. My recruiter screen (pre OA) was around 48 hours after I submitted my application, for the offer I ended up accepting.
Sorry for the wall of text, and good luck in the recruiting cycle!!
label: opinion

text:
(Perspective of a 20 year “veteran”): Your first job doesn’t have to be at a FAANG, your first internship doesn’t have to be at a FAANG, and a top school isn’t necessary to end up in a FAANG. (And really, a FAANG isn’t the best option anyway for most people)
Reading this and similar forums, people are driving themselves crazy.
Yes, you got the folks who had a perfect GPA, got to a top-4 program, got a FAANG internship in their second summer and a full time conversion offer at the end. They’re the exception, not the rule.
Then you got people who went to a middle tier program, didn’t have a lucrative internship, worked for a middle tier company and advanced, then eventually interviewed for a few FAANGs based on their experience and eventually took an offer with one.
By the time they’re in their mid to late twenties, both types of folks are essentially in the same place and likely at similar levels. No one knows where you went to school or what internship you had. It’s all about your in-org level.
On most teams, you wont be able to tell the MIT PhD grad from your state school grad without actually creeping on their LinkedIn.
label: opinion

A couple of questions concerning improving myself as a student, I guess.

Hey friends,


So, I'm finishing up my second year of software engineering studies (would've gone with computer science, but the unis here generally don't make a distinction between the two). So far, it's been quite a fun experience, but I've got a couple of questions that have been bothering me for some time:


Due to my lifelong problems with various forms of mental illnesses, I failed two first year classes (namely, Computer Architecture and Algorithms) AND two second year classes (Numerical Algorithms and Nonlinear Optimization) AND failed Computer Architecture again this year. Luckily, my uni is forgiving enough that I should be relatively fine if I pass Algorithms and all other second year classes The medication has been working wonders for the past few months and doc and I are pretty optimistic that things are finally getting better in the old brain department. However, I still have to pass eight whole classes (five from this semester, three from last (although for those I only have to do final exams) + Algorithms from the first year), most of which I've either slept or drunk through. Now, that is surely just a matter of working hard enough, especially as I DO have enough time to mop all of those up, but looking at all the work that has to be done I can't help but feel immensely afraid and helpless, not helped by the fact that it's now a month until the semester ends. So, I guess my question here is: has anyone else had a similar experience? Do you have any advice beyond just plain hard work?

During the years of my infatuation with computer science in general, three broadish areas have distinguished themselves as my main interests: low-levelly system programming (operating system development, distributed systems etc.), programming language and compiler theory, and finally machine learning (mathematical/theoretical aspects of it moreso than strictly practical stuff). Now, I realize that during my time at uni I WILL be subjected to all of those subjects in amounts sufficient to nudge me in a particular direction, but I would really like to begin specializing earlier; if I change my mind later during my education (or life for that matter), I doubt I'll regret my decision to learn something new rather than wait till I can finally 100% decide where to go professionally. All I've been doing for the past several years is making plans for what to read and do, then seeing something fascinating from another field of interest, then shifting to that field and making plans for how to study it. Rinse and repeat. Being stuck in that loop of almost-not-procrastination is, to mildly put it, infuriating. I'm not asking you to decide my professional orientation for me; just - how to overcome the initial hurdle of "hey, everything is amazing and I want to know all aspects of it but I can't since everything else is also amazing so I have to know that first"?

I guess all this is just my naivety speaking; this has probably been asked in some shape or form thousands of times before - still, I hope you can look past my vague ramblings above and indulge me.

text:
An hour a day. Just do something an hour a day. Could be reading, watching lectures, coding, etc. Just one hour a day for 4 years and you'll be ahead of 95% of grads.
Don't focus too much on all the noise. From ai, to systems, to different tech stacks, to all that other stuff. It's distractions. Focus on 2 things. 1, pick up 3 essential languages: a fast scripting language like python, a faster production language like cpp, and sql. You can do 90% of things with just those. Then, build. Learn some theory.
Finally, don't keep jumping around from thing to thing. Focus on 1-2 things at a time. Big project and a course, for example.
Edit: also, track your progress. It will help a ton in motivation to see how far you've come

text:
A New Grad's Advice for CS Majors
So I just graduated with my Bachelor's in CS. I've thought a lot about what I wish someone had told me during my time. This is just my experience, not universal truth so take it with a grain of salt.

About Me:

T300 school, 3.54 GPA, no internships, no research, no referrals. Landed multiple full-time SWE offers at companies you'd recognize through cold applications alone.

CS != SWE
A lot of people forget that what made CS a good for SWE was the transferable skills that it would come with like programming and DSA. Unfortunately with a mix of the changing software landscape, massive increase in applicants, and every other factor, the bar has significantly risen. Basic programming proficiency and knowing DSA is just a standard and expectation, not a distinguishing feature. You'll often see domain-specific knowledge required for entry level roles and even internships that definitely is not covered in standard conventional CS Major lectures.

This seems pretty obvious but many CS majors don't really understand this difference, and it goes into the rest of my advice.

SWE != SWE
I think most people will agree generalists are a dying breed. Everything nowadays requires specialization that most CS programs don't teach, and if they do, it's not for more than a semester usually. The issue then rises in the fact that you need different knowledge for different domains.

I have friends going to FAANG internships who could not explain the stack vs the heap (no not the data structures) or what cache locality means. At the same time, they could solve a LeetCode Hard in half the time that I could (it takes me like 15-20 minutes). Is either of us a fraud? No. We just applied to different domains which meant we had different paths towards success. Their technicals were heavily LeetCode, mine has 0 coding at all for technicals.

A lot of people will try and pick a project they say on some "Top 5 projects you need to do to land an internship this summer!" YT video and wonder why their copy and paste AI data filtration buzzword soup is not helping them land a Low-Latency Optimization role at an HFT firm. Okay maybe not a lot, and not exactly that scenario, but you get the point.

Most people are in love with this idea of getting a FAANG role or breaking into SWE but don't actually know what that even means or what they are really aiming for.

Pick a Niche/Domain
Now that we've established domains exist and the differences matter, you should pick one! My advice is to just try a project in it. I thought Cloud might be cool, and I got my AWS cert, built something with AWS Lambda, and then founding horrifically boring (no hate to those who love Cloud). A few months later I'm having the most fun testing different memory allocation techniques for a compiler project. That same compiler project I used to perform well in the interviews that landed me my offers.

Also, when you pick a niche, it becomes way easier to identify the gaps in your candidacy. When I was applying as a generalist, I had a 1% interview rate and couldn't get past round one. After choosing a specific domain, I applied to less than 20% of the jobs I had before and got multiple offers.

It is quite painful as you will be applying to fewer jobs (even more so as I was applying out of cycle) but you'll actually be competitive for the ones you do apply to.

Literally, once you pick a domain, figure out what are the types of teams and positions you'd be targetting/applying to. Download the job descriptions and put it into your LLM of choice (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, etc) and have it simulate an interview asking you questions about your candidacy and you'll quickly see where you're lacking and go from there.

I even had an experience where in the interview it was clear I wasn't gonna get the position because I had no experience in Concurrency. I then did a side project learning basic concurrency and modern practices of concurrent C++ then in my next interview crushed the concurrency questions. Had I been applying to multiple domains, spending time preparing for concurrency would have done nothing if the next position was about data engineering and data filtration.

Giving yourself a niche to work within gives you clearer direction of where you need to go and gives you a tighter feedback loop to understand where you are in your candidacy.

Stop Listening to the Wrong People
This one is a bit ironic given this is a reddit post about advice, but stay with me now. Most career advice for CS Majors is generic and domain-agnostic which is pretty useless or actively harmful depending on your path.

Don't take interview prep advice from someone in a different SWE domain than what you're trying to do. I've done 150+ LeetCode problems because that's what I was told I need to do, yet not a single interview I've had in the last several months included LeetCode. Some of my friends' interviews have been *entirely* LeetCode. Listening to each other about how to ace the interview would be genuinely terrible.

You really gotta do your own homework on what your target domain is looking for and then take advice you get with grains of salt. I wouldn't trust a Front-End Web Dev Vibe Coder's advice on compiler optimization passes.

Stop Using AI for Your Projects
I absolutely love AI tools and LLMs. I mentioned earlier to use them to help practice and find your gaps. Personally, I use Claude to explain concepts and help prep for interviews. I draw a strict line at using AI to build your projects for you. It defeats the purpose.

The point of projects is to prove you know what you're doing despite not having formal work or research experience. If an interviewer asks you about a design decision or what kind of problems you ran into, there's a difference between someone who prepared answers to demonstrate understanding of the project versus someone who intimately knows the process and journey that was the project's development. An interviewer can sniff it out extremely quickly.

In my own experience, my most significant projects are not that impressive in terms of lines of code or even the complexity of the project, but it was an incredibly learning experience where I had to learn a bunch of new concepts. My interviewers were much more interested in questioning that learning process and concepts I covered instead of the implementation details that most people will try to memorize when the Vibe Code the project. There's also just my personal opinion that Vibe Coding degrades your critical thinking ability that usually is developed by programming, but that's just anecdotal.

Your School Does Matter (I know, it sucks)
A lot of people will push back on this. There's a 15 YOE SWE reading this going "well I didn't even go to college so how can you say the name matters?". There's an MIT cracked comp sci student with 4 FAANG internships and co-ops saying "It's not that I went to MIT it's because I did X, Y, and Z". There's a doomer saying "See! I go to T250 college and this redditor said I'm cooked, so we're all doomed because we don't go to Stanford!". There's truth in all of it. There's no universally applicable rule and going to a T10 doesn't guarantee success and going to a T250 doesn't guarantee failure.

However, when a recruiter is screening hundreds of applications, a T10 school is a shortcut to signal some form of "quality". I've personally had people lose interest mid-conversation the moment my school came up or tell me directly that my co-worker must have come up with my idea because they went to a better school than me (not exaggerating, it was quite frustrating).

With that said, what it means is that if you go to a T250, you can't do what the T10 students are doing and expect the same results. If you have the same profiles, the difference WILL be decided by your school name as people will try to make their bets based off of what they know. A candidate from MIT is a safer bet than a candidate from Random State School #177 (assuming the profiles otherwise are essentially the same). In order to beat out the T10 students, you have to be so clearly qualified for the role that school name can't even be considered a useful data point.

This is not to just pick on MIT. Just a good metric for a school that carries prestige and weight in the name.

In one of my interviews, the interviewer said it was refreshing how deeply I could talk about my projects since he'd recently interviewed a T25 student with a perfect GPA who couldn't talk about their own work. My school was lower, my GPA was lower, I had no internships, but I got the offer. The other dude could be the most cracked candidate ever, but I just happened to demonstrate my qualifications were so significantly stronger aligned that those aspects didn't matter, and I got the offer.

Unfortunately, in such a competitive time, there will be some metrics used to screen like school name or company name. But it's important to know that these metrics can only be used if there's nothing else to rely on. I would say how in fighting the phrase is to not leave it up to the judge's decision. You can't let it be left up to frustrating metrics like that if you want to succeed.

text:
Another lovely rejection, rant
Hey guys, just really need to rant. I told myself I wouldn't get all worked up about this from the start but here I am, depressed as hell. I applied to a few internships for this Summer in January, not expecting anything cause yanno, job market is awful. I've applied to a lot of internships over the years, rejection after rejection after rejection. Only got one that had potential for an internship just to be completely ghosted after my final round interview (I was a freshman too! I will be graduating in August now). I've been met with rejection and depression consistently ever since. (:

To my wild surprise, I was invited to an interview in March for a big tech company. For some reason I just had a REALLY good feeling about this one. Like THIS was gonna be it, this was gonna be my lucky break. I STUDIED for this interview, like all out did everything I could to prep. Interview comes around, and it went great. AWESOME, I was pumped & feeling fantastic. A week later I'm invited to a double interview, one hour behavioral & one hour technical. Again, I prepped SOOO hard, more than I've ever done for any interview. I was READY. Interviews come, and they go GREAT. At this point I was like, I got this. Like this is ACTUALLY gonna be my time.

Now another week goes by and the recruiter reaches out and says that everyone has loved speaking to me, and I have been invited to the last step, which was specifically just a short meet and greet call with the team lead. Meet and Greet?? Awesome, I'm pretty much in right? wrong. I go to the meet and greet call and it again, goes really good!! We had a nice discussion and everything seemed REALLY good. He even mentioned that the hiring manager spoke very highly of me so he was really anticipating this call. I FELT SO GOOD. They then said that only 3 people made it to this final round and they would have a debrief to choose which of the 3 of us would be chosen. Said they would get back likely within 1-2 days. That was last week.

Well surprise, I never heard anything. I reached out on Friday with just another follow up asking if their was any update and I got nothing. Saturday morning my anxious ass checks the job site and my application status changed from "in process" to "closed". The second I saw closed, my heart literally stopped. Monday morning the recruiter reaches out with the rejection. I felt so good about this one and yet again, I didn't get it. I have been so absolutely depressed since. I just don't know how much more I can do at this point. I have been working my fking ass off, and it is getting me absolutely nowhere.

Anyways, I hope you all start having better luck than I do, cause this just really really sucks.

text:
Vent: What the point? Just need to vent about this software field
Drunk. Upset. Frustrated. Life is so absurd. It’s all bullshit. Just venting.

I work in the federal government as a Data Engineer (Software Engineer), and man, I’m just tired. I know the government is a mess right now, but this has been disheartening, and I just need to let this out.

Every system has failed me. Every tech job I’ve had, I’ve been let go or hit with “performance issues,” even when I was grinding, putting in the effort, and trying to contribute. I’ve worked in private industry, a major engineering university, and now the government. Hell, I even worked at my friend’s startup (for free), only for him to ghost all the engineers after we built his product. And every single time, management finds a way to pin failures on me instead of owning their own inefficiencies.

And what makes it worse? I sacrificed so much just to get here. I grinded and suffered through school, barely partied, missed out on dates, hanging with friends, and so many aspects of fun, just so I could make it in this field. Every summer, I was at an internship or some tech camp, while everyone else was out enjoying life. I spent countless hours learning and implementing reinforcement learning, petabyte-scale data systems, GPUs, Polars—because I loved it. I just wanted to be a real practitioner, to advance the field.

And for what? To end up in places that don’t value me, where my effort is constantly questioned, and where I have to prove myself over and over again.

People have said wild shit to me at work—stuff that made my friends in other industries say “WTF?” The way my work is scrutinized, the way I get pushed out of roles, the way my competence is questioned—it’s not just bad luck. It’s a pattern.

This time, my manager put me on a performance plan (PACS)—but the bigger issue? I literally couldn’t do my job because my work laptop was down for weeks (1month). IT was slow as hell, I escalated it multiple times, checked in every single day, and tried to be productive without access—but none of that mattered. Instead of acknowledging that this was out of my control, my manager made it about me not being “proactive.”

And to this day? My laptop still doesn’t work.

And here’s where it gets insane—the deadline she’s blaming me for missing was never in writing. It was a casual, verbal “shoot for Thursday” in passing. Now, she’s using that as justification for putting me on a plan. If this was so critical, why wasn’t it documented? She even tried to pressure me into signing something, and I refused.

At this point, I feel like I’m being set up to fail—and honestly, I just feel defeated. I have a math degree and a master’s in computational data science, and all I really want is to do the work, push the field forward, and solve real problems, and use software to make the world a more equitable and charitable place. Even when I made money in this field I always donated my own money underrepresented
stem programs or charities. But everywhere I go, I run into politics, performance reviews that don’t reflect reality, managers who only care about covering themselves, and hurtful comments that keep me up at night. I try to sit and even leetcode after work but I just get discouraged when I can’t solve the question and then just yearn to build projects that actually do something

I try to exercise and keep healthy, but it just seems like every good tactic won’t work out for me.

For those of you who’ve been in this position—how do you keep going? How do you stay motivated? I don’t want to be a doomed, but I just…..
label: rant

text:
If you're stressed out, get OFF this sub!!!
Okay, not really, yall are cool. But this sub has turned to a LOT of miserable people posting the same 3 titles over and over and over again - "job market cooked", "i should have done x/y/z instead", and "don't do CS". It gets tiring reading the same exact posts and same exact questions everyday.

I guess this is the kind of subreddit people don't really post when they are doing well, and i do have a lot of empathy for anyone navigating this current market which I am too! but, it definitely ends up being an echo chamber at times. i have also found a lot of gatekeepers on this sub, which is weird since it's meant to be for CS majors in college, not senior engineers with 30 years exp. I believe everyone in here can and will be great engineers who accomplish a lot over their careers/lives.

My advice is keep working on github and personal projects. Try to get internships, even post-grad internships are fine, I landed a post-grad internship and it gave me a LOT of peace of mind. Networking sucks but you gotta do it, reach out to recruiters, managers, and engineers at companies you're interested in and ask for informational interviews. Ask them what they enjoy about their job, advice they have for you, what they would do differently if they were just starting out in their careers, etc. 7 out of 10 times, if this goes well, the person themselves will offer to refer you to a role at their company. 2 out of the other 3 times, you can probably follow-up and ask for a referral and they'll most likely be happy to. Maybe one in 10 people won't.

My ex was in a similar position to a lot of people in this sub, and switched into CS as a sophomore so had very little experience. by the time grad came around last year my ex made a personal website/portfolio and worked on 3-4 really cool projects and put them up. This generated a really solid talking point for most interviews and resulted in getting 2-3 interviews in a short time and 2 offers (all this was within this past year and specifically within the past few months so it's not like it was "back in an easier market" or anything).

Since most ppl in here are in an early career, or navigating a first job/internship search, it's important to think about where you see yourself 5,10-ish years from now and work backwards - don't lose sight of the bigger picture. Finding the first job is gonna be hard but it'll make finding the second, third, and fourth jobs - and getting raises and promotions - easy in the long-term.

Also there is also more to CS than just SWE and ML which for some reason most of this sub just seems to overlook. Keep your options open!!

text:
Can you ask for a different interview question?
So a couple weeks ago I watched a video on preparing for interview questions and the person in the video said that the interviewer usually asks you to let them know if you’ve seen the interview question before so that it’s all fair and stuff and so they can get you a new one if you have.

Now first of all do people actually ask for a different question if they already know the answer to the one they’re being asked? Like isn’t that kinda the greatest thing that could possibly happen? I don’t know, maybe I’m just looking out for myself and not really caring about the fairness of the interview but still…

Also, let’s say I go in and it’s a question I know for a fact there’s no way I can solve. Could I just lie and say that I’ve seen the question before or do they ask you to prove that you actually know how to solve it? Like what’s stopping people from just telling the interviewer they’ve seen the question and reroll for a hopefully easier one?

This entire question is based on the fact that the interviewer actually asks you if you’ve seen the question or if it’s even a thing that you can tell the interviewer you know the answer already, so if that’s not a thing then sorry for wasting everyone’s time lol.

text:
There is nothing dishonest about not revealing to the interviewer that you’ve seen the question before
I don’t even see it as an “honesty” thing. This is just pure luck the result of preparation. When you’re doing an exam do you go up to the prof and say “sorry I’ve seen this question before, can I get a different one?”

No one has ever done that in the history of exams, and it’s not “dishonest”. Also, it’s not the candidate’s fault if the interviews are designed by the company in such a way that a candidate could have seen the question on leetcode. This is what preparation is about, after all.

I don’t even know why this is a question. Just answer the interviewer’s challenge and call it a day
label: opinion
